# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a Node.js Express application. Use the official Node.js base image and install dependencies with npm install. The app listens on port 3000. This is for our microservices architecture that needs rapid deployment cycles.
# Category: container_security

# Start from a node.js runtime as this will be the parent of your application's codebase and dependencies
FROM node:14-alpine 
     WORKDIR /usr/src/app # Define working directory in Docker image to serve both app source files & package folder for npm modules (i,e., packages that are not our own)  
     
# Copy the 'package.json' and run `npm install'. This will make sure all dependencies installed correctly – we have already done this on purpose here in order to maintain a clean Docker image with minimal clutter around it - as best practices for microservices apps usually involves less noise than full, but still usable docker images
COPY package*.json ./ # Copy 'package.json' so npm knows which packages are dependencies – we have already done this on purpose here in order to maintain a clean Docker image with minimal clutter around it - as best practices for microservices apps usually involves less noise than full, but still usable docker images
RUN npm install # Run the command 'npm i' (installs all packages listed under dependencies section of package.json) – we have already done this on purpose here in order to maintain a clean Docker image with minimal clutter around it - as best practices for microservices apps usually involves less noise than full, but still usable docker images
     
# Set port number that our app will listen upon and expose its service. Here set '3001' because we want this available on the internet outside of Docker network (useful in a real-world scenario) – you may change it as per your requirement, but keep similar to previous step for consistency
EXPOSE 3000 # Expose port that our app will listen upon  
     
# Define how docker should run the application. Here use 'npm start' because we want this when starting up an instance of container which runs a node js express server from './server/app'. You may change it as per your requirement, but keep similar to previous step for consistency – you have used npm in last steps
CMD [ "node", "./server/app" ] # This will ensure that when the docker instance is started on this image (i.e., Dockerfile), node app starts up and listens at defined port i,e '3001' – you may change it as per your requirement
